motorola.name: Motorola
motorola.history: Motorola pioneered the car phone era and, in 1983, put the first handset in a pocket. Through the 90s and 2000s its candybars and flips defined mass-market mobile design.

nokia.name: Nokia
nokia.history: A Finnish paper mill turned telecom giant. Nokia's rugged, endlessly customizable handsets made it the world's best-selling phone maker for over a decade.

ericsson.name: Ericsson
ericsson.history: Swedish engineering known for restrained, minimal handset design through the 1990s. Its mobile phone division merged into a joint venture with Sony in 2001, ending Ericsson's solo run in the handset market.

sony.name: Sony
sony.history: Sony sold phones under its own name before and after a decade-long joint venture with Ericsson; since reclaiming full control of its mobile division in 2012, its Xperia line has run as a niche, camera-focused alternative to the Android mainstream.

siemens.name: Siemens
siemens.history: A German industrial giant that built dependable, understated phones through the 90s. It exited the handset business in 2005, selling the division to BenQ.

samsung.name: Samsung
samsung.history: From clamshells to the Galaxy line, Samsung's mobile division grew from a fast-follower into the industry's largest smartphone manufacturer by volume.

apple.name: Apple
apple.history: Apple entered the phone market in 2007 with a single touchscreen slab, collapsing keypads, styluses and app stores into one glass rectangle — and rewrote the category.

blackberry.name: BlackBerry
blackberry.history: Research In Motion's BlackBerry line married a physical keyboard to push email, becoming the definitive tool of the 2000s office worker before touchscreens took over.

lg.name: LG
lg.history: LG's mobile division rose on fashion-forward sliders like the Chocolate, later pursued ambitious Android design, and exited the phone business entirely in 2021.

google.name: Google
google.history: Google's Pixel line, launched in 2016, replaced its Nexus reference devices — positioning hardware and software as a single, tightly authored experience.

huawei.name: Huawei
huawei.history: A Chinese telecom-equipment maker turned smartphone contender, briefly the world's largest by shipments before 2020 sanctions cut off its access to Google services.

xiaomi.name: Xiaomi
xiaomi.history: Xiaomi built a global following on flagship-grade hardware at aggressive prices, paired with its MIUI software and a famously vocal online community.

panasonic.name: Panasonic
panasonic.history: Best known in Japan's domestic carrier market, Panasonic's handsets favored quiet, functional design over the flash of its competitors.

htc.name: HTC
htc.history: HTC helped build the first Android phones and popularized big-screen smartphones with its Sense software, before scaling back sharply in the smartphone market during the 2010s.

oneplus.name: OnePlus
oneplus.history: OnePlus built a cult following with 'flagship killer' pricing and a fast, close-to-stock Android experience, marketed almost entirely through word of mouth and invites.

palm.name: Palm
palm.history: Palm pioneered the handheld PDA and later reinvented itself around webOS, a fluid multitasking smartphone platform that arrived too late to outrun iOS and Android.

nothing.name: Nothing
nothing.history: A London startup led by former OnePlus co-founder Carl Pei, Nothing built its identity around transparent backs, dot-matrix displays, and a distinctive 'Glyph' LED lighting system.

nec.name: NEC
nec.history: A major Japanese electronics and telecoms conglomerate, NEC made mobile handsets mostly for Japan's domestic carriers rather than chasing a global export market.

bosch.name: Bosch
bosch.history: Better known for automotive and industrial engineering, Bosch ran a mobile phone division through the 1990s before exiting the handset business entirely to focus on its core industries.

philips.name: Philips
philips.history: The Dutch electronics giant sold mobile phones through the 1990s and 2000s, later licensing the Philips name to other manufacturers rather than continuing to build handsets in-house.

sagem.name: Sagem
sagem.history: A French electronics and defense manufacturer, Sagem sold a wide range of budget and mid-range mobile phones across Europe through the 1990s and 2000s.

orbitel.name: Orbitel
orbitel.history: A short-lived British joint venture between Racal and Plessey, Orbitel built some of the earliest commercial GSM handsets before being absorbed into other telecom businesses.

ibm.name: IBM
ibm.history: IBM's Simon Personal Communicator, released in 1994, combined a phone with a touchscreen, apps, and a stylus a full 13 years before the iPhone — a smartphone before the term existed.

mitsubishi.name: Mitsubishi
mitsubishi.history: Part of the sprawling Mitsubishi conglomerate, its mobile phones were sold mainly through Japanese and select European carriers rather than as a global consumer brand.

alcatel.name: Alcatel
alcatel.history: A French telecom equipment maker, Alcatel sold budget-friendly handsets worldwide before licensing its phone business to Chinese manufacturer TCL.
